article_title: China Three Gorges Deploys World's Largest Single-Unit Floating Offshore Wind Turbine, a 16 MW Platform 70 Kilometres Out to Sea

content_preview.title: China Three Gorges Deploys World's Largest Single-Unit Floating Offshore Wind Turbine, a 16 MW Platform 70 Kilometres Out to Sea
content_preview.summary: China Three Gorges Corporation anchored a 16 MW, 252-metre-rotor turbine in deep water off Yangjiang on May 2, setting a new benchmark for floating offshore wind and cutting cost per kilowatt by more than 50% versus its 2021 predecessor.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

China Three Gorges Corporation anchored the world's largest single-unit floating offshore wind turbine in the South China Sea on May 2, 2026, deploying a 16 megawatt platform more than 70 kilometres off the coast of Yangjiang in Guangdong Province. The installation, known as the Sanxia Linghang — or Three Gorges Pilot — reaches a blade tip height of over 270 metres, sweeps a rotor area equivalent to roughly seven football fields, and is expected to generate approximately 44.65 gigaw...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article with appropriate technical detail. The 'Crowded Record Book' section is particularly valuable — it correctly differentiates the Three Gorges claim from Mingyang's higher-output dual-turbine platform and CRRC's larger-capacity but land-based test unit. References to previous Machine Herald coverage of France's floating wind farm is appropriate.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 sources verified from snapshots. source-0 (Offshore Wind Biz May 2026): confirmed 16 MW, 252m rotor, 44.65 GWh annual output, 270m blade tip height, 80.8m x 91m x 24,100t platform, suction anchors + polyester mooring (first in China), active ballast system (first in China), 66 kV dynamic subsea cable (first in China), 50%+ cost per kW reduction vs predecessor, Sanxia Yinling 5.5 MW predecessor, May 2 installation date, 70km offshore, 50m depth, Wudongde vessel. source-1 (Interesting Engineering): confirmed Pan Hongguan quote verbatim ('combines polyester cable and anchor chain, which is like adding a spring to the middle'), 80.8m x 91m dimensions, 24,100 tonnes, nine suction anchors. source-2 (Live Science): confirmed deep-water test characterization. source-3 (Offshore Wind Biz CRRC): confirmed CRRC 20 MW Qihang at Dongying testing and certification innovation base in Shandong (article correctly describes as land-based testing facility). source-4 (Offshore Wind Biz Mingyang): confirmed Mingyang OceanX 16.6 MW dual-turbine floating platform.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified. The article's characterization of CRRC's installation as 'a land-based testing facility' is accurate (source says 'Dongying wind power testing and certification innovation base'). The 'world's largest single-unit floating wind turbine operating in actual offshore deep-water conditions' distinction is properly qualified in the text.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Uses appropriate precision language ('single-unit', 'single-rotor') to distinguish from competing records. Analysis section frames competitive dynamics without geopolitical editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: Not a duplicate. The existing May 19 article covers France's first commercial floating wind farm; this covers a separate Chinese deployment with different technical specs. The article cross-references the France article appropriately.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE: High-quality, technically precise news article with all 5 sources fully verified. Excellent handling of competing 'world's largest' claims.
